data.title: Eurohold, Fibank bolster Sofia equity indices
data.description:
SOFIA (Bulgaria), March 28 (SeeNews) - All five indices of the Bulgarian Stock Exchange (BSE) posted gains on Friday, with the blue-chip SOFIX recording the biggest one, of 0.80% to 910.69 points, bourse data showed.

The top performers in the benchmark index were energy and insurance group Eurohold Bulgaria [BUL:EUBG], which advanced by 2.11% to 1.94 levs, and First Investment Bank, or Fibank, [BUL:FIB], which grew by 2.00% to 4.08 levs.

Both companies also spurred on the BGBX40, which tracks the 40 most traded shares on the Sofia bourse, and the BGTR30, in which companies with a free float of at least 10% have equal weight. The largest increase in the BGBX40 index was booked by Industrial Capital Holding [BUL:HIKA], which added 3.36% to 2.46 levs.

Overall, the BGBX40 went up 0.56% to 173.89 points, whereas the BGTR30 zoomed 0.26% to 881.86 points.

The BGREIT index, which tracks seven real estate investment trusts, ended the session 0.19% higher at 214.28 points after a 0.98% climb in Bulgarian Real Estate Fund REIT [BUL:BREF] to 4.14 levs.

Lastly, the BEAMX, which tracks the ten most highly capitalised growth companies, swelled 0.79% to 93.48 points on the back of a 15.75% surge in payment services provider Paysera Bulgaria [BUL:PAY] to 7.35 levs.

Total turnover on the Sofia bourse declined to 4.27 million levs ($2.36 million/2.18 million euro) on Friday from 5.26 million levs on Thursday.

Diversified group Sila Holding [BUL:HSI] generated the highest turnover, of 775,000 levs, as 250,000 shares changed hands. The stock closed flat at 3.10 levs.

(1 euro = 1.95583 levs)

data.title: Fed's Daly: Inflation progress has been flat, dents confidence in rate cut view
data.description:
San Francisco Federal Reserve Bank President Mary Daly on Friday said inflation data published earlier in the day confirms her decreased confidence in her baseline expectation that two interest rate cuts this year are a "reasonable" projection.

Government data on Friday showed underlying inflation rose 0.4% in February - the most in more than a year - and a survey from the University of Michigan showed consumer inflation expectations rose.

"One hundred percent of my focus is on what's happening with inflation," Daly told Reuters on Friday in a follow-up call from an interview on Thursday. "The progress there hasn't been so decisive that I'm comfortable starting any kind of rate path declines right now."

With monetary policy restrictive, she said, the most likely path for the economy is slowing growth, a cooler labor market, further progress on inflation - and, eventually, rate cuts. But the plateau on inflation progress also illustrates the rising risk that the economy veers from that benign path, she said.

While true stagflation - stalled economic growth paired with high inflation - is still an unlikely tail risk that business contacts do not expect, she said, she does see a bigger chance of other scenarios.

"Six months from now you could find that growth in the labor market has remained relatively solid, but inflation has just not come down as expected. And so that would mean that adjusting the rate path wouldn't be warranted because we still are fighting to get inflation towards our 2% goal," she said.

On the other hand, she said, business and consumer sentiment "have gotten a little shaky of late; it could end up being that translates into changes in hiring plans, etcetera that could slow the economy more that we would like to see, and put it into a situation where there's more challenges and that would call for maybe a different posture."

data.title: US drillers cut oil and gas rigs for first time in three weeks, Baker Hughes says
data.description:
U.S. energy firms this week cut the number of oil and natural gas rigs operating for the first time in three weeks, energy services firm Baker Hughes said in its closely followed report on Friday.

The oil and gas rig count, an early indicator of future output, fell by one to 592 in the week to March 28. (RIG-USA-BHI), (RIG-OL-USA-BHI), (RIG-GS-USA-BHI)

Baker Hughes said this week's decline puts the total rig count down 29 rigs, or 5% below this time last year.

Baker Hughes said oil rigs fell by two to 484 this week, while gas rigs rose by one to 103.

In the Permian basin in West Texas and eastern New Mexico, the nation's biggest oil-producing shale basin, the rig count declined by three rigs, cutting the total count down to 297, the lowest since February 2022.

data.title: Former Morgan Stanley Advisor Gets 12 Years in Prison for $5.6 Million Fraud
data.description:
By Kenneth Corbin

A former Morgan Stanley advisor has been sentenced to 12 years in prison for a long-running fraud that bilked investors out of nearly $5.6 million.

Jesus Rodriguez, 46, pleaded guilty in November to four criminal counts, including wire fraud and aggravated identity theft. His lawyer, Rebecca Reyes, declined to comment on the sentencing on Rodgriguez's behalf.

From May 2018 through August 2021, Rodriguez arranged for a series of fraudulent transfers from Morgan Stanley accounts into bank accounts he was able to gain access to, using the funds for a variety of expenses, including the purchase of a Lamborghini.

He facilitated those transfers through "materially false pretenses, representations, and promises," the Justice Department says.

In all, Rodriguez's fraud amounted to $5,554,968.10 in losses for Morgan Stanley, according to the DOJ.

In a statement, Morgan says it notified law enforcement authorities after allegations of Rodgriguez's theft came to light and cooperated with investigators as they probed the matter. The firm says it has reimbursed clients for all the losses they incurred through Rodriguez's fraud.

"Morgan Stanley reached out to clients and amicably resolved claims with impacted clients," it says.

Rodriguez has long been on the radar of financial regulators. Finra, the brokerage sector's self-regulatory organization, barred him from the industry in November 2021, three months after he resigned from Morgan Stanley. Rodriguez's record in the online database BrokerCheck indicates that his resignation came after allegations surfaced of his "use of client line of credit for his personal benefit."

Last January, the Securities and Exchange Commission brought civil charges against Rodriguez in a case that remains pending.

Morgan Stanley faced its own regulatory probe involving allegations that it failed to prevent and detect the fraud and theft carried out by Rodriguez and three other advisors. In December, Morgan agreed to pay $15 million to settle those allegations without admitting or denying wrongdoing.

Compounding Rodriguez's legal troubles was his failure to report the funds he embezzled on his income tax returns over the years of his fraud. One of the four counts to which he pleaded guilty was "making and subscribing a false income tax return," according to authorities.

data.title: APPLIED ENERGETICS, INC. SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Applied Energetics, Inc., a leader in advanced laser and photonics systems, has released its annual Form 10-K report, detailing the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's activities in the defense and commercial markets, particularly focusing on its fiber-based ultrashort pulse (USP) laser technologies and directed energy systems.

Financial Highlights

Applied Energetics, Inc. reported the following key financial metrics for the fiscal year:

  • Revenue: $2.43 million, a decrease of approximately $205,000 or 7.8% from the previous year due to a contract modification.
  • Cost of Revenue: $1.48 million, an increase of approximately $842,000 or 132.10% due to higher costs of materials, supplies, and direct labor.
  • General and Administrative Expenses: $9.51 million, an increase of approximately $738,000 or 8.4% primarily due to higher salaries and employee benefits.
  • Selling and Marketing Expenses: $0.375 million, a decrease of approximately $9,500 or 2.5% due to continued business development activities.
  • Research and Development Expenses: $0.239 million, an increase of approximately $5,300 or 2.3% due to increased labor and material costs.
  • Net Loss: $9.17 million, an increase of approximately $1.82 million or 24.8% compared to the previous year, primarily due to increased general and administrative expenses and research and development costs.

Business Highlights

Applied Energetics, Inc. has made significant strides in its business operations, focusing on advanced laser and photonics systems:

  • Technology and Capabilities: The company specializes in fiber-based ultrashort pulse (USP) laser technologies, holding 26 patents and 8 patents pending. These technologies offer size-weight-power reductions compared to traditional continuous wave laser technology.
  • Directed Energy Systems: The company's directed energy technologies, including Laser Guided Energy (LGE®) and Laser Induced Plasma Channel (LIPC®), are designed for defense platforms to deliver high-intensity, ultrashort pulses of light to disable or destroy targets. These technologies have applications in national security and commercial markets.
  • Recent Developments: In March 2025, Applied Energetics moved to the next phase of its strategic collaboration with Kord Technologies, Inc. to integrate an advanced pulsed laser system with Kord’s FIREFLYTM High Energy Laser Weapon System. The company also opened a new Battle Lab in February 2025 to test and demonstrate emerging laser technologies.
  • Ongoing Business Operations: Applied Energetics executed a contract with the Department of the Navy, Office of Naval Research, to develop a high-peak and high-average power USP optical system. The company also has a Phase II Small Business Technology Transfer contract with the U.S. Army to develop an IR laser system.
  • Strategic Plan and Analysis: The company aims to increase the energy, peak power, and frequency agility of USP optical sources while decreasing their size, weight, and cost. Applied Energetics is developing partnerships with leading laser and optics institutes in the U.S. to enhance its research and development capabilities.
  • Market for Technology: The company targets unmanned systems and directed energy systems markets, leveraging its proprietary fiber-based architecture for dual-use technologies. The directed energy sector is anticipated to grow significantly, with the U.S. Department of Defense's directed energy spending increasing from $500 million in 2017 to over $1.695 billion in 2023.
  • Facilities: Applied Energetics operates a 26,800 sq ft facility at the University of Arizona Tech Park, which includes a Class 1000 cleanroom, multiple integrated laser labs, and a new Battle Lab for manufacturing and testing advanced lasers.
  • Competition: The company faces competition from major defense contractors and international companies in the directed energy systems market. However, Applied Energetics believes its unique USP technologies and renewed U.S. Government interest in directed energy applications will support its growth.
  • Future Outlook: Applied Energetics plans to continue expanding its management and science teams, pursue strategic corporate acquisitions, and explore equity financing opportunities. The company is optimistic about the growing opportunities in directed energy applications and aims to become a significant developer in the USP marketplace.

Strategic Initiatives

Applied Energetics, Inc. is focused on expanding its technical capabilities and strategic collaborations:

  • The company has entered into a strategic collaboration with Kord Technologies, Inc. to develop and integrate advanced pulsed laser systems.
  • Additionally, the company has opened a new Battle Lab to enhance its manufacturing and integration capabilities for advanced lasers.
  • The company is also pursuing strategic corporate acquisitions in related fields and technology to enhance its intellectual property portfolio.
  • Capital Management: The company raised approximately $6 million through a private placement of common stock in early 2025 to support its operations and strategic initiatives. Applied Energetics is actively exploring equity financing opportunities to support its growth and development plans. The company has a working capital deficit of $67,639 as of December 31, 2024, and is focused on managing its cash flow and capital resources to ensure continued operations.
  • Future Outlook: Applied Energetics plans to increase the energy, peak power, and frequency agility of its USP optical sources while reducing their size, weight, and cost. The company anticipates strong funding for the directed energy community and aims to leverage its existing patent portfolio to become a significant developer in the USP marketplace. The company is also monitoring geopolitical events and inflationary pressures that could impact its supply chain and cost structures, and it is prepared to adjust its strategies accordingly.

Challenges and Risks

Applied Energetics, Inc. faces several challenges and risks in its operations:

  • The company faces challenges in securing government contracts due to potential changes in US government spending and the establishment of the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE), which aims to reduce federal spending, including defense budgets. This could impact the company's ability to secure contracts and affect cash flows.
  • The company faces significant risks related to its financial condition, including substantial doubt about its ability to continue as a going concern due to recurring losses and negative cash flow. The need for additional financing is critical, and failure to secure it could lead to modifications or curtailment of operations.
  • Economic and geopolitical factors, such as inflation, supply chain challenges, and changes in government spending, pose additional risks. The company is heavily reliant on US government contracts, which are subject to termination and funding uncertainties.
  • Intellectual property protection is crucial, but the company may face challenges in defending its rights. Cybersecurity threats also pose a risk to operations and business development efforts.
  • Management highlights the challenges of securing additional contracts and the impact of supply chain disruptions and inflation on costs. The company is focused on expanding its management and science teams to enhance its capabilities and address these challenges.
  • The strategic plan includes increasing the energy and power of its laser systems while reducing size and cost, aiming to expand into commercial and biomedical markets. However, the company remains dependent on government contracts, and any changes in government priorities or budget allocations could significantly impact operations.
  • The company is exposed to market risks, including fluctuations in government spending and potential changes in defense priorities. The limited number of potential customers in the defense sector and the competitive landscape pose additional challenges. The company must navigate regulatory requirements and potential changes in policy that could affect its ability to secure and perform on government contracts.

data.title: EU wheat hits new lows as exports and weather weigh
data.description:
Euronext wheat hit fresh multi-month lows on Friday, pressured by export concerns and improving northern hemisphere crop weather, though prices pared their losses by the close.

May wheat (BL2K5) on Euronext settled 0.2% up at 218.75 euros a metric ton, steadying after an earlier one-year low of 215.00 euros.

December wheat (BL2Z5), widely used as a benchmark for new-crop prices, ended down 0.3% at 225.25 euros, after reaching its lowest in nearly four months at 222.50 euros.

Traders were also looking ahead to U.S. planting and stocks data on Monday and sweeping U.S. tariffs planned from Wednesday encouraged prices to consolidate.

Chicago wheat extended losses to its weakest level in eight months.

Forecast rain in the U.S. and the Black Sea region has eased worries about crop stress in major wheat belts.

"At this point, it seems that very little weather risk premium is priced in, with funds maintaining their large net short positions," CM Navigator analyst Donatas Jankauskas said in a note.

"But as always, weather will ultimately have the final say."

In Europe, a rebound in the euro against the dollar and sparse international demand have disappointed traders.

"The stronger euro is bad news for export prospects as French stocks in my view need to be reduced before the new harvest and the West EU does have a price advantage over the Black Sea after this week's Euronext fall," a German trader said.

The Eid holiday in Muslim countries next week was expected to curb import activity in major wheat markets in North Africa and the Middle East.

Russian 11.5% wheat for April/May delivery rose about $2 a ton to $244-$247 a ton FOB, with French prices about $4-$6 a ton cheaper depending on Euronext moves, traders said. Ukrainian, Romanian and Bulgarian wheat was about $3-$5 cheaper than Russian but still currently above west EU prices, traders said.

Ukrainian wheat, including new crop, was meanwhile offered at low prices inside the EU, traders said.

Ukrainian 11% protein milling wheat was quoted at 243-245 euros a ton including prompt delivery to northern Italy. Ukrainian new crop feed wheat was around 211-213 euros a ton including delivery to northern Germany.

In France, soft wheat crop ratings held steady last week and remained above last year's rain-hit levels, data from farm office FranceAgriMer showed.

data.title: Update: Wolfspeed Gets $192.1 Million in Tax Refunds Amid Reported Debt Refinancing Hurdle
data.description:
(Updates with Bloomberg report on convertible bonds starting in third paragraph.)

Wolfspeed said Friday it received $192.1 million in cash tax refunds through the Section 48D advanced manufacturing tax credit, including $186.5 million for fiscal 2023 and 2024, plus interest.

Wolfspeed also said in a statement it is actively exploring financial alternatives for its convertible notes while engaging with lenders, including Apollo Global Management and Renesas Electronics, and is in talks with government entities to secure funding and support US semiconductor manufacturing and supply chain initiatives.

The company is struggling to refinance its $575 million convertible bonds due next year, and the chipmaker is working with JPMorgan Chase (JPM) on the issue, Bloomberg News reported, citing people with knowledge of the matter.

JPMorgan Chase and Wolfspeed declined to comment, Bloomberg said.

The tax refund was part of an expected $1 billion of total Section 48D refunds, with over $600 million expected in fiscal 2026, Wolfspeed said in the statement, adding that it expects its cash balance to be around $1.3 billion by the end of its fiscal Q3, including the recently received 48D cash tax credits.

The company reaffirmed its fiscal Q3 guidance, expecting a non-GAAP net loss of $0.76 to $0.88 per diluted share on revenue of $170 million to $200 million. Analysts surveyed by FactSet expect a loss of $0.82 on revenue of $185.7 million.

Wolfspeed shares tumbled 50% in recent Friday trading.

data.title: S&P 500 Falls 1.5%, Nasdaq Plummets 2% Amid Inflation and Tariff Fears
data.description:
Wall Street tumbled Friday as hotter-than-expected inflation data sparked concerns about the Federal Reserve's next moves. The S&P 500 was on track for a weekly decline, erasing gains from its recent rebound.

At midday, the S&P 500 dropped 1.5%, the Dow lost 1.3%, and the Nasdaq Composite plunged 2.1%.

Bond markets saw a shift as investors moved away from equities. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) dipped to 4.28%, down 7 basis points, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) fell to 3.95%, slipping 5 basis points.

According to the Federal Reserve's Core PCE Price Index the most reliable inflation measurement grew 0.4% during one month beyond the projected rise of 0.3%. Year-over-year, it rose 2.8%, also slightly above forecasts. The inflation data made people guess the Fed would wait longer before easing its interest rates to protect price stability.

Market participants felt extra stress because of their concerns about import taxes. The planned economic sanctions from April to May made investors fear worsening market instability. On Thursday the market's uneven trends showed investors they need to stay cautious since inflation and trade risks still threaten them.

data.title: FDIC shifts stance on digital assets and will allow financial institutions to engage in crypto activities without getting prior sign-off
data.description:
The U.S. Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation is taking a new approach under the Trump administration toward digital assets and is turning back standards requiring financial institutions to notify the agency before engaging in crypto-related activities.

In a statement released on Friday, the FDIC said it would be rescinding old guidance and providing a new one to clarify that its supervised institutions can "engage in permissible crypto-related activities" without getting the agency's go-ahead beforehand.

"With today's action, the FDIC is turning the page on the flawed approach of the past three years," said FDIC Acting Chair Travis Hill in a statement on Friday. "I expect this to be one of several steps the FDIC will take to lay out a new approach for how banks can engage in crypto and blockchain related activities in accordance with safety and soundness standards."

The FDIC previously warned that crypto activities could pose a risk to the U.S. banking system. In recognizing those risks, the agency released a Financial Institution Letter in April 2022, urging its supervised institutions to inform the agency about their current and intended crypto-related activities.

On Tuesday, the FDIC also looked to put an end to "reputational risk" as a way to supervise banks following criticism from some in the crypto industry who say they have been blocked from key financial services. Separately, another U.S. agency, which regulates and supervises national banks and federal savings associations, also rescinded previous digital asset restrictions earlier this month. The Office of the Comptroller of the Currency clarified that crypto-related activities are allowed in the federal banking system.

This all comes after President Donald Trump has signaled a friendlier approach to crypto than the previous Biden administration. Trump has criticized the Biden administration's approach to banks and crypto, saying on March 7 at a White House Summit that the previous administration "strong-armed banks into closing the accounts of crypto businesses and entrepreneurs, effectively blocking some money transfers to and from exchanges, and they weaponized government against the entire industry."

Bo Hines, executive director of the President's Council of Advisers for Digital Assets, said the FDIC's move was "another big win."

data.title: Saudi Arabia's capital markets regulator approves flynas IPO
data.description:
Saudi Arabian budget airline flynas, which is backed by billionaire Prince Alwaleed Bin Talal, is planning to float on Riyadh's bourse, the kingdom's markets regulator said on Friday.

The nearly twenty-year-old company is going ahead with plans to sell a 30% stake, according to a statement by the Saudi capital markets authority, joining a raft of companies that have flocked to Gulf bourses in recent years.

The flynas prospectus will be published prior to the start of the subscription period, the statement added.

The carrier is set to debut after a years-long boom in the airline industry following the COVID-19 pandemic, and as Saudi Arabia has made tourism key to its domestic economic agenda.

The listing would be only the third by a Gulf airline after the United Arab Emirates' Air Arabia and Kuwait's Jazeera Airways , and the first in nearly two decades.

Launched as Nas Air in 2007, flynas serves over 70 destinations with more than 60 Airbus A320 and A330 jets. The airline is targeting a fleet of 160 aircraft by 2030.

Among its shareholders are Kingdom Holding, the Saudi Arabian investment company founded by Prince Alwaleed, who was once the country's best-known international investor, buying up holdings in companies like Citigroup, Twitter and Four Seasons.

Saudi Arabia's PIF sovereign wealth fund bought around 17% of Kingdom Holding in 2022.

The prince, a member of the kingdom's vast ruling family, was detained in 2017 amid a sweeping purge of elites by de facto ruler Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman, but released the following year after striking a confidential agreement with the government.

Saudi Arabia is spending billions of dollars overhauling its economy to create new industries and jobs and develop a vibrant private sector to reduce the country's dependence on oil rents.

The kingdom, which attracts tens of millions of religious pilgrims a year to holy Muslim sites in Mecca and Medina, has revamped its tourism industry to attract non-religious tourists.

Tourism is a major pillar of the economic overhaul and the government is establishing a new state-owned airline, Riyadh Air, to start operations next year. Other major airlines in Saudi Arabia are Saudia and flyadeal, both state-owned.

data.title: Beef Prices Are Rising. How Restaurants Could Feel the Squeeze.
data.description:
By Evie Liu

While egg prices have come down from record highs, the cost of beef — a key protein on most American tables — continues to rise. That could not only dent consumer wallets, but also raise input costs for restaurants, forcing them to either raise prices or risk lower profits.

Over the past few years, severe droughts in cattle-producing regions across the U.S. have diminished grazing lands, leading ranchers to rely more on supplemental feed. This has in turn driven up the cost of feed. Other production expenses, including labor and energy, have escalated as well.

What's more, high interest rates since the pandemic have raised borrowing costs for operating loans, which many ranchers rely on for purchasing cattle and maintaining herds.

All these factors have pushed ranchers to reduce the size of their herds. As of January, the U.S. cattle herd has dwindled to 86.7 million head, marking its lowest level since 1951. As demand remains strong, beef prices have been climbing at a fast pace since 2021.

In February, prices for beef and veal rose 2.4% from a month ago and 7.6% year-over-year, according to the latest consumer price index from the Bureau of Labor Statistics. That has far outpaced the overall CPI's 0.2% monthly increase and 2.8% gain from a year ago.

Prices for ground beef reached an average of $5.63 per pound in February, up 9.7% from a year ago, while the average price for all uncooked beef steaks rose to $10.87 per pound, up by 3.2%.

"There is not enough cattle for the next two, three years," said Christopher Pappas, CEO of Chefs' Warehouse, a wholesale distributor of specialty foods, on the firm's latest earnings call. "I don't think we're going to get much relief on that."

To make it worse, the Trump administration recently imposed a 25% tariff on imported goods — including beef — from key trade partners such as Canada and Mexico. Currently, there is an exemption on the tariffs until early April, but if the levies go into effect, it would raise the costs of internationally sourced beef as well.

The U.S. also imports a significant volume of beef and veal products from Australia, and some industry experts worry the Trump administration might impose tariffs on Australian beef as well.

While that previously seemed improbable given current challenges in the domestic beef supply, the perspective seems to have shifted, wrote J.P. Morgan analyst Lucas Ferreira in a note last week. If enacted, the tariffs could further exacerbate the upward pressure on beef prices.

"This development represents an additional headwind for the beef market, potentially affecting both consumer prices and supply dynamics," wrote Ferreira.

Restaurants, especially those heavily reliant on beef dishes, have been feeling the pressure from rising beef costs.

Bloomin Brands, the owner of Outback Steakhouse, said on the latest earnings call that it expects commodity costs to rise between 2.5% and 3.5% in 2025, driven in large part by beef inflation, which is expected to be in the mid-single digits range.

The company said it has locked in about 76% of its beef costs through futures contracts, which could hedge against rising prices and help smooth out the blow.

Likewise, Texas Roadhouse said it expects commodity inflation in 2025 to come between 3% and 4%, with the majority driven by beef.

The company said it has locked in prices for about 40% of its input basket, but mostly for the first half of the year. Management expects to see a fairly consistent inflationary outlook of 3% to 4% from the second quarter of 2025.

At a March conference, Shake Shack CFO Katherine Fogertey called beef inflation one of the biggest risks for the burger joint. Beef represents 25% to 30% of Shake Shack's ingredients and packaging costs, according to the firm.

"We don't hedge our beef costs," she told investors. "If there was even more elevated inflation than what we've baked into our guide, there's not a lot that we can do to offset that."

Some companies will be more insulated from the beef headwinds than others. Beyond Meat, which sells plant-based meat substitutes, sees rising beef costs as an opportunity for it to shine.

At the firm's latest earnings call, CEO Ethan Walden Brown called what's happening in animal agriculture — expensive eggs caused by bird flu and rising beef costs due to dry weather — an existential threat that cannot be easily fixed by the government or food industry.

"You can have some beef out on the pasture, but you have to have far, far less," he said, "That's not going to provide a global protein solution."

Sprouts Farmers Market, known for catering to consumers with higher income and special dietary needs, also wasn't too fussed about rising beef costs.

"If you're a vegan, it really doesn't matter what the price of ground beef is," said Sprouts CFO Curtis Valentine at a recent conference. "You're going to continue to shop your vegan products."

Write to Evie Liu at evie.liu@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

(Cboe CA: LML) ("LML" or, the "Company") (formerly, Golden Share Resources Corporation) is pleased to announce that it has received final listing approval from Cboe Canada Inc. ("Cboe") and that the trading of its common shares will commence at market open on March 31, 2025 under the symbol "LML".

Ken Johnson, President and CEO of LML, stated, "The commencement of trading on Cboe marks a significant milestone for our company. Cboe's global exchange infrastructure across the US, EU, UK, and Australia, will help expand our reach to a broader investor base in the future. We take immense pride in making our shares publicly available and believe that this increased visibility and liquidity will be instrumental in accelerating our growth and maximizing value for our shareholders."

In connection with LML's listing on Cboe, the Company has prepared and filed on its SEDAR+ profile at www.sedarplus.ca a listing statement in the form prescribed by the Cboe (the "Listing Statement"). The Listing Statement, together with the financial statements, MD&A and technical reports as incorporated by reference therein, provides full details of the Company, its business, management, capital structure and risk factors.

About Lipari Mining Ltd.

LML is a corporation incorporated under the law of Canada. LML's head office is located at 517 Rua João Chagas Ortins de Freitas, Sala 301, Lauro de Freitas, Bahia, Brazil CEP:42.710-610, and its registered office is located at Suite 2400, 40 King Street West, Toronto, Ontario M5H 3Y2. LML's principal business is the acquisition, exploration and development of resource properties for the mining of diamonds with a focus in Angola and Brazil.

In Angola, LML owns a 75% equity interest in Tchitengo Mining - Sociedade de Prospecção e Exploração Mineira, Ltda. ("Tchitengo Mining"), which controls a 100% interest in the Tchitengo diamond project ("Tchitengo Project"), located in the principal diamond producing district in northeastern Angola. The Tchitengo Project encompasses 30 known kimberlite deposits, with the Tchiuzo kimberlite being the most advanced in terms of development.

LML also owns 100% of the Braúna diamond mine, located in the State of Bahia, Brazil. The Braúna mine commenced commercial production in July 2016 and has produced 1,190,701 carats of natural rough diamonds from 6.54 million tonnes of kimberlite mined from the Braúna 3 kimberlite for an average production grade of 18.2 cpht to December 31, 2024. Operations are now focused on the transition of the mine from an open pit operation to an underground operation.

data.title: Cybersecurity, IT infrastructure firms likely to escape federal budget cut impact
data.description:
Key points:
  • S&P 500, Dow off >1%; Nasdaq slides >2%
  • Comm svcs off most among S&P sectors; utilities only gainer
  • Euro STOXX 600 index falls ~0.8%
  • Dollar down, crude falls; gold up; bitcoin down more than 3%
  • U.S. 10-Year Treasury yield falls to ~4.26%

CYBERSECURITY, IT INFRASTRUCTURE FIRMS LIKELY TO ESCAPE FEDERAL BUDGET CUT IMPACT

Federal IT departments face budget cuts of about 10% in 2025 and the following years, according to brokerage Rosenblatt, with new projects being delayed and existing ones facing increased scrutiny.

Programs that don't match the new administration's goals, especially diversity and inclusion initiatives, will likely see the biggest cuts, along with wasteful projects, consulting fees, and per-person software licenses. Traditional software installed on local servers is particularly at risk as budgets tighten.

While there would be near-term disruption in IT contracts pipeline and new project demand, the federal IT market requires much modernization and next-generation process automation and could present a significant growth market in the medium to long term, Rosenblatt analysts say.

With security threats persisting globally, cybersecurity is also likely to remain a key priority, the analysts note.

The revamp could present an opportunity for cybersecurity firms like Palo Alto Networks , CrowdStrike and Fortinet as well as infrastructure software companies like Datadog and Dynatrace .

Palo Alto, for instance, is well-positioned to capitalize on the demand for total platform cybersecurity solutions, Rosenblatt says, noting that a majority of the company's business in the federal government is focused on firewalls.

These companies also have diversified revenue sources, with federal spending contributing less than 10% of business, insulating them from a significant near-term hit.

Meanwhile, major consulting firms like Booz Allen , Deloitte, and Accenture could see steep budget cuts.

(Kritika Lamba)

data.title: Robinhood Halts NCAA Bets in New Jersey as States Push Back on Prediction Markets
data.description:
By Nick Devor

Robinhood will no longer allow New Jersey residents to trade event contracts based on the NCAA college basketball tournaments after it received a cease-and-desist letter from the state's gambling regulator Thursday night, the company said.

Kalshi, the prediction market firm Robinhood partnered with to offer the event contracts on its brokerage app, also received a cease-and-desist from the New Jersey Division of Gaming Enforcement. Kalshi declined to comment on its response to the order.

Barron's has learned that Washington state is considering similar action: "We are looking into these types of 'predictions markets' as a whole and are unable to discuss the specifics of any active investigations further," an attorney at the Washington state Gambling Commission said in a statement.

The New Jersey letters, addressed to Tarek Mansour and Vlad Tenev, the CEOs of Kalshi and Robinhood, respectively, give the firms until 11:59 p.m. on Friday to cease "all sports wagering activities targeting New Jersey residents."

Each company "is listing unauthorized sports wagers for individuals located within the state of New Jersey," copies of the letters obtained by Barron's state. "Failure to comply with this directive will result in the Division taking further enforcement actions."

Event contracts are intended to be investment instruments that allow traders to hedge against events that could have a negative impact on their positions, and are built around yes/no questions like "Will there be a recession this year?" Prediction markets have begun to use their platforms to offer contracts based on future sports outcomes, effectively allowing its users to bet on the events, as Barron's recently reported. Kalshi also has contracts based on political events and pop culture.

Some state regulators have argued that event contracts should be subject to the same state laws and taxes that apply to traditional sports betting. The gambling industry has taken a similar view.

The prediction markets counter that event contracts are federally regulated by the Commodity Futures Trading Commission, putting them outside of state jurisdiction.

"The new cease-and-desist letter from New Jersey tees up an imminent lawsuit that could alter the course of sports betting legalization in the U.S.," says Ryan Rodenberg, a professor at Florida State University whose research focuses on sports gambling policy.

In 2018, a New Jersey lawsuit led to the U.S. Supreme Court overturning a national sports betting ban, clearing the way for states to legalize sports betting.

New Jersey's latest actions follow a cease-and-desist order that Nevada's Gaming Control Board sent to Kalshi in early March. Kalshi has received an extension on the deadline to halt its operations there. Massachusetts' secretary of state subpoenaed Robinhood this week as part of an investigation into its event contracts.

Robinhood hasn't cut off New Jersey residents from its event contract offerings altogether: contracts focused on the fed-funds rate remain accessible in the app's prediction markets hub.

The state's cease-and-desist letters cite its Sports Wagering Act, "which only permits licensed entities to offer sports wagering to patrons located in New Jersey."

In statements to Barron's, Kalshi and Robinhood note that their event contracts are already regulated at the federal level.

"Kalshi believes in the value of regulation and operates under the comprehensive oversight of the Commodity Futures Trading Commission," a Kalshi spokesperson said.

"Event contracts offered by Robinhood Derivatives are federally regulated by the CFTC and offered through CFTC-registered entities, assuring that retail customers can access these prediction markets in a safe and regulated manner," the Robinhood spokesperson said.

While Robinhood has pulled its contracts off the table for New Jersey residents, the firm told Barron's that "we do not believe these contracts run afoul of any state laws."

The New Jersey regulator says Kalshi and Robinhood are violating the state's constitution, which bans wagering on college sporting events held in the state. The first game of the men's tournament's Sweet 16 round was played last night at the Prudential Center in Newark, New Jersey. The arena will host an Elite Eight matchup on Saturday.

Write to nicholas.devor@barrons.com

data.title: Basis bids flat as industry awaits USDA reports
data.description:
Spot basis bids for hard red winter wheat were unchanged in the U.S. Plains on Friday amid slow trade as farmers awaited the U.S. Department of Agriculture's Monday reports before making selling decisions, dealers said.

* The USDA's planting report will be issued together with quarterly estimates of U.S. grain stocks in one of the most closely watched data releases of the year for grain markets.

* Wheat basis bids have held steady this week.

* Farmers and industry players are also monitoring rainfall over parched wheat-growing regions of the U.S. Plains.

* Roughly 14% of the U.S. winter wheat crop is affected by drought as of Tuesday, the U.S. Department of Agriculture's Drought Monitor reported.

* Forecast rain for U.S. and Russian wheat belts and a low volume of U.S. wheat export sales reported on Thursday also pushed wheat futures lower.

* Most-active KC hard red winter wheat futures (KWv1) were last down 15 cents to $5.51-1/4 per bushel around midday.

data.title: Paraguay GDP Annual Growth Accelerates in Q4
data.description:
Paraguay's gross domestic product grew by 3.6 percent year-on-year in the fourth quarter of 2024, accelerating from a downwardly revised 3.1 percent expansion in the previous period.

This growth was driven by increased output in construction (13.6 percent vs 6.0 percent in Q3), and services (4.8 percent vs 3.2 percent), while the contraction for utilities eased (-7.9 percent vs -9.4 percent).

However, growth eased for manufacturing (3.1 percent vs 3.8 percent), and livestock, forestry, fishing and mining (2.7 percent vs 13.4 percent), while output contracted for agriculture (-4.3 percent vs 0.9).

On a quarterly basis, Paraguay’s GDP soared by 10.2% in the fourth quarter over the previous quarter.

data.title: Heard on the Street: CoreWeave Delivers A Verdict on the AI Boom-and on Itself
data.description:
By Asa Fitch

CoreWeave's lackluster stock-market debut Friday offered further proof the artificial-intelligence trade is losing steam. It also said something about CoreWeave itself.

The company makes money by buying AI chips from Nvidia and then renting out access to them. That business boomed over the past two years: Startups and tech giants trying to outduel each other in AI turned to CoreWeave for additional computing power. Revenue climbed from $229 million in 2023 to $1.9 billion last year.

For better or worse, much of that growth has come from Microsoft, which accounted for 62% of revenue last year. But CoreWeave's role as an overflow supplier to Microsoft makes it especially vulnerable if the AI boom gets less boom-y.

CoreWeave has long-term contracts with Microsoft, but there's no assurance they will be renewed - and the tech giant could try to renegotiate them in harder times. CoreWeave chief Michael Intrator said in an interview that Microsoft wasn't going to back away from contracts, and he expected it would continue coming to CoreWeave for computing power even as it built up more of its own.

In addition to growing its data center footprint, Microsoft is developing its own custom AI chips and would save money by shifting toward using its own data centers for AI work.

"Operating data centers for these giants at a significant cost disadvantage is not sustainably profitable," New Street Research analyst Pierre Ferragu said in a note.

For now, CoreWeave benefits by being one of relatively few avenues for stock-market investors to put money into a fast-growing AI story. Throw in rising losses, a growing debt pile, and an unusually large cash-out by its founders ahead of the listing, however, and investors have some reason to pause-even if the broader AI boom doesn't fizzle.

This analysis comes from the Journal's Heard on the Street team. Subscribe to their free daily afternoon newsletter here.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Winnebago Shares Fall After Truist and BMO Cut Price Targets
data.description:
By Josh Beckerman

Winnebago Industries shares declined after Truist Securities and BMO Capital reduced their price targets.

The stock was 7% lower Friday at $34.95 and is down about 26% this year. Truist lowered its target to $50 from $58 while BMO decreased its target to $60 from $70.

The RV and boat maker said Thursday that revenue for the second quarter ended March 1 fell 12% to $620.2 million, reflecting a reduction in average selling price per unit related to product mix.

Winnebago revised full-year revenue guidance to $2.8 billion to $3 billion, compared with its previous outlook of $2.9 billion to $3.2 billion, citing "stubborn interest rates, inconsistent consumer sentiment, and dealers that continue to push inventory levels lower."

Write to Josh Beckerman at josh.beckerman@wsj.com

data.title: Leet Technology Inc. SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Leet Technology Inc., a company that has transitioned from manufacturing Breath Alcohol Ignition Interlock Devices to focusing on the eSports industry, particularly in Southeast Asia, has released its annual 10-K report. The report highlights significant financial growth, strategic initiatives, and the challenges the company faces as it continues to expand its presence in the rapidly growing eSports market.

Financial Highlights

  • Revenue: $715,288,000, an increase of 125.3% from $317,413,000 in 2023, mainly attributed to the white label project with Smart Communications, Inc. and revenue from Matchroom Mini-app solutions.
  • Gross Profit: $141,356,000, a significant improvement from a loss of $325,903,000 in 2023, due to increased revenue and decreased cost of revenue.
  • Net Loss: $894,000, a substantial decrease of 99.9% from $1,383,447,000 in 2023, primarily due to increased revenue and reduced general and administrative expenses.
  • Basic and Diluted Loss Per Common Share: $(0.00), improved from $(0.01) in 2023, reflecting the significant reduction in net loss.

Business Highlights

  • Corporate Transition: The company transitioned from manufacturing Breath Alcohol Ignition Interlock Devices to focusing on the eSports industry, particularly in Southeast Asia, following the acquisition of Leet Technology Limited in November 2020.
  • eSports Platform Operations: The company operates Matchroom.net, a community eSports gaming platform launched in January 2019, which offers a range of gaming and digital entertainment services tailored to the Asia Pacific community.
  • Product Portfolio: The company offers MAVERICK, a modular-based white label platform, providing customizable solutions to ICT partners and OTT companies to enhance brand engagement and monetization.
  • Geographical Focus: The company targets emerging markets in Southeast Asia, South Asia, and the Middle East, focusing on mobile-centric eSports tournaments and partnerships with mobile network operators.
  • Sales and Marketing Strategy: The company employs a subscription model for tournament passes, leveraging partnerships with mobile network operators for payment integration and expanding reach through digital marketing and influencer collaborations.
  • Partnerships and Collaborations: In 2022, the company launched white label partnerships with Smart Communications in the Philippines and the Axiata Group across Bangladesh, Cambodia, Indonesia, and Malaysia, enhancing its platform offerings.
  • Future Outlook: The company plans to expand its B2B model and Maverick product offerings across Southeast Asia, South Asia, and other continents, focusing on integrating new features and enhancing user engagement through strategic partnerships with telcos and OTT providers.

Strategic Initiatives

  • Securing Additional Financing: Leet Technology Inc. is focusing on securing additional financing to sustain operations, as current cash levels are insufficient. The company is pursuing strategic partnerships and sponsorship agreements to enhance revenue streams, particularly through its esports tournament management and white label solutions.
  • Capital Management: The company has issued Series B Convertible Preferred Stock to related parties, which includes dividend rights and potential redemption by the company. It has also engaged in stock cancellations to rectify previous over-issuances. The company relies on advances from related parties and directors to manage liquidity, as evidenced by the $605,518 generated from financing activities in 2024.
  • Future Outlook: Leet Technology Inc. plans to continue relying on financial support from stockholders and related parties while seeking additional financing. The company aims to enhance its capital structure through strategic equity management and is exploring opportunities to list on a national securities exchange to improve market presence and access to capital.

Challenges and Risks

  • Early-Stage Company Risks: As an early-stage company in a developing industry, there is a risk of insufficient capital to achieve growth, failure to develop profitable products, and significant fluctuations in operating results.
  • Going Concern: The company has a history of net losses and a working capital deficiency, raising substantial doubt about its ability to continue as a going concern.
  • Competition: The eSports industry is highly competitive, with new entrants and established companies potentially having greater resources. This competition could impact profit margins and market share.
  • Technological Changes: Rapid technological advances and changing consumer preferences in the eSports industry could render the company's offerings obsolete if it fails to keep pace.
  • Regulatory Risks: Compliance with various laws and regulations is necessary, and failure to do so could result in litigation and penalties.
  • Market Volatility: The market price of the company's common stock is volatile, influenced by various factors including operating results, economic conditions, and market trends.
  • Management Strategies: The company is pursuing additional financing to support operations, but there is no assurance of success. Management is focused on expanding its platform offerings and enhancing its white label product, Maverick, to better serve telecommunications partners and increase revenue streams.

SEC Filing: Leet Technology Inc. [ LTES ] - 10-K - Mar. 28, 2025

data.title: Brazil Economy Adds More Jobs than Expected
data.description:
The Brazilian economy added 431,995 formal jobs in February of 2025, way above market expectations of 250 thousand.

The services sector led February's growth with 254,812 jobs, followed by industry (+69,884), commerce (+46,587) and construction (+40,871), while agriculture posted gains of (+19,842).

São Paulo contributed the most, adding 137,581 jobs, with services accounting for the bulk.

The average real salary for new hires was R$2,205.25, a decrease of R$79.41 (-3.48%) from January.

Over the past 12 months, Brazil generated 1,782,761 jobs.
